misconceptionSummary: Students calculate missing sides by assuming the cut-out corner has sides equal to the full shape's sides.

lines.text: L-shape: outer 9 cm wide, 7 cm tall; cut-out 4 cm wide
Student: missing vertical = 7 cm (same as full height)  ✗
Missing vertical depends on cut-out depth, not full height

steps.narration: The student assumed the step-in on the L-shape goes the full height of seven centimetres. But the cut-out has its own depth — the missing vertical is the overall height minus the cut-out's depth, not the full height.

visuals.ruleText: In each direction, all parallel segments sum to the same total as the overall span
visuals.subText: Missing segment = overall span − sum of all other parallel segments in that direction

steps.narration: The key rule: in each direction, the segments on one side sum to the same as the segments on the other side. Use that to calculate any missing length without guessing.

Horizontal: the full width is nine; the cut-out is four — the remaining step is five. Vertical: the full height is seven; the cut-out depth is three — the step is four. Now add all six boundary sides.

visuals.expression: Missing side = overall span − known parallel segment
visuals.label: Parallel segments must sum to the overall span in each direction

steps.narration: Calculate missing sides using the overall span minus known segments. Never assume a missing side equals the full dimension.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: assuming a missing vertical side equals the full height of the shape. Use overall height − cut-out depth. Parallel segments must total the overall span.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Missing sides on a compound shape are found by subtraction — overall span minus known segments in that direction. Never assume a missing side equals the full dimension.
